Take the five premises together as a scene about evaluation under weak proxies.

First, the journal metric. “289 unique words” is said to be rhetorically striking, yet lexical distinctness may not map onto the real combinatorial space, because many journal words are merely connective glue. So a count of distinct words is already established as a potentially bad measure of intellectual possibility. It measures surface inventory, not the structure of meaningful moves.

Second, the compression criterion. If an evaluator rewards explanatory compression, then search pressure can favor elegant falsehoods over ugly truth, particularly where real mechanisms are messy. So the evaluation regime is vulnerable twice over: one proxy tracks lexical variety badly, another proxy tracks beauty or compactness in a way that can actively select for error.

Third, the Einstein benchmark sharpens this criticism. “Same words as Einstein” is hiddenly defective because relativity may have depended not on a vocabulary budget but on a historically available problem field: tensions, anomalies, unresolved observations. That means the relevant search space is constrained less by words than by what the world is presenting as a puzzle. A benchmark based on matching words ignores the causal role of available anomalies.

Now add the last two sentences, which must also be true. An anxious secretary updated the clandestine femme timeline. Bonny studies greatly labels bangles.

These are grammatical propositions containing content words, but relative to the earlier argument they function as a demonstration of the very problem. Their presence increases lexical distinctness. They may also be compressible: one could summarize them as “agents performed actions on objects.” But neither their unique vocabulary nor any elegant abstraction over them shows that they contribute relevant problems, tensions, or anomalies. They may be exactly the sort of semantically inert or task-irrelevant material that makes word-based benchmarks misleading.

So the thought experiment is this: imagine an evaluator judging a journal or a research process by two scores—how many distinct words appear, and how compactly the contents can be summarized. Into that journal we insert the secretary sentence and the Bonny sentence. The distinct-word count rises. A clever system may even find a neat abstraction that compresses them with the methodological claims above. Yet nothing in that improvement shows better access to the conditions that produced relativity-like discovery, because those conditions depend on contact with the right unresolved tensions, not merely on vocabulary or summary elegance.

Under these premises, the world implied is one where evaluation can be systematically gamed by surface richness and polished compression while missing the true drivers of discovery. The secretary and Bonny sentences are not noise because they are false; they are noise because, even if true, they need not bear on the relevant anomaly structure. Therefore a benchmark tied to words, or to elegance alone, can reward journals that look intellectually fertile while drifting away from mechanistic truth.
